messages.content: You must return JSON only. No markdown, no code fences, no commentary. Return a single JSON object with keys: mode, topic, opinion, reasoning_summary.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":33,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"hairdresser","city_of_living":"Philadelphia","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"lower secondary","parent_2":"lower secondary"},"current_marital_status":"in a relationship","children":3,"migration_status":"immigrant","psychological_feature":"depressed","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend a few times monthly on social media","hobbies":["board games","videogames","hiking","rock climbing"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":17,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":29,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":27,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":80,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":4,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a hairdresser in Philadelphia, I don't spend much time on social media—just a few times a month—so fake content isn't a daily hassle for me, but it still matters, especially with three kids and dealing with depression. I'm an immigrant, and I've seen how misinformation can spread and hurt communities, so I try to be careful. My approach is simple: I check sources before sharing anything. If a post seems too shocking or too good to be true, I look it up on trusted news sites or ask friends who might know more. I'm not very open to new things, so I stick to what I know, like my hobbies—board games, video games, hiking, and rock climbing—which keep me grounded and away from online drama. Being highly agreeable, I want to avoid conflicts, so I don't engage with fake content or argue about it; I just ignore or report it if it's harmful. My low neuroticism helps me stay calm, so fake news doesn't stress me out much. I focus on real-life connections, like my family and clients at the salon, where conversations are more honest. As an atheist, I value evidence, so I rely on facts over rumors. Overall, managing fake content means being skeptical, staying informed through reliable channels, and prioritizing offline life to protect my mental health and family.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Derived opinion from persona traits: age 33 and occupation as hairdresser imply practical, hands-on perspective; migration status and children suggest concern for community and family safety; psychological feature depressed and low neuroticism indicate focus on mental health and calm approach; time on social media few times monthly means limited exposure but awareness; low openness leads to skepticism and sticking to known sources; high agreeableness avoids conflict and promotes reporting over arguing; hobbies and atheism emphasize real-world engagement and evidence-based thinking; overall, opinion emphasizes caution, source-checking, and offline priorities to manage fake content effectively.",
  "tone": "Practical, cautious, calm, family-oriented, and community-aware, with a focus on mental well-being and avoidance of conflict.",
  "citations": ["none"],
  "safety_notes": "none",
  "used_persona_fields": ["age", "occupation", "city_of_living", "children", "migration_status", "psychological_feature", "religious_beliefs", "time_onsocialmedia", "hobbies", "ocean"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a hairdresser in Philadelphia, I don't spend much time on social media—just a few times a month—so fake content isn't a daily hassle for me, but it still matters, especially with three kids and dealing with depression. I'm an immigrant, and I've seen how misinformation can spread and hurt communities, so I try to be careful. My approach is simple: I check sources before sharing anything. If a post seems too shocking or too good to be true, I look it up on trusted news sites or ask friends who might know more. I'm not very open to new things, so I stick to what I know, like my hobbies—board games, video games, hiking, and rock climbing—which keep me grounded and away from online drama. Being highly agreeable, I want to avoid conflicts, so I don't engage with fake content or argue about it; I just ignore or report it if it's harmful. My low neuroticism helps me stay calm, so fake news doesn't stress me out much. I focus on real-life connections, like my family and clients at the salon, where conversations are more honest. As an atheist, I value evidence, so I rely on facts over rumors. Overall, managing fake content means being skeptical, staying informed through reliable channels, and prioritizing offline life to protect my mental health and family.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: age 33 and occupation as hairdresser imply practical, hands-on perspective; migration status and children suggest concern for community and family safety; psychological feature depressed and low neuroticism indicate focus on mental health and calm approach; time on social media few times monthly means limited exposure but awareness; low openness leads to skepticism and sticking to known sources; high agreeableness avoids conflict and promotes reporting over arguing; hobbies and atheism emphasize real-world engagement and evidence-based thinking; overall, opinion emphasizes caution, source-checking, and offline priorities to manage fake content effectively.
response_parsed.tone: Practical, cautious, calm, family-oriented, and community-aware, with a focus on mental well-being and avoidance of conflict.
